title-group.article-title: Single Stage Isolated on Board EV Charger With PFC

abstract.p: As EVs are gaining importance day by day, significant research and development is ongoing to make the charging process convenient. In onboard EV chargers, power factor correction, isolation, and efficiency are the main concerns. This article presents a single-stage isolated onboard charger to charge the battery in CC-CV mode. The front-end rectifier is removed, and an AC-to-high-frequency AC converter is used. The output of this converter is rectified and controlled for CC-CV charging while maintaining unity power factor. The 3 kW charger is designed and operates in CC-CV mode. The proposed system is validated using simulation results.

institution-wrap.institution: Department of Science and Technology, Government of India

funding-group.funding-statement: Authors would like to acknowledge Department of Science and Technology, Government of India for the financial support under EU-India RE-EMPOWERED Project (File No.: DST/TMD/INDIA/EU/ILES/2020/50).

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: People are shifting from conventional IC engines to electric vehicles, as they provide a clean and cheaper option for transportation. The charger of EVs plays an important role in the process of developing and adapting the EV system. The charger not only is responsible for transferring power from the grid to the vehicle, but it also has to take care of the grid and battery health. The charger should provide galvanic isolation, be able to improve the power factor, and transfer power efficiently. [1].

The power factor correction unit of a charger is responsible for maintaining unity power factor and keeping total harmonic distortion within the limits specified by IEC standards [2]. In most popular EV charger configurations, a front-end diode bridge rectifier is used, followed by a boost converter. This combination is known as boost PFC, which is the simplest, most reliable, and most cost-effective option for lower power ratings [3]–[4]. Several other topologies, such as interleaved, SEPIC, Cuk, and totem-pole PFCs, have been developed in recent years to further improve the power factor correction process efficient. [5] [6] [7] [8] [9]. For onboard EV chargers, a two-stage conversion process is generally followed. The different topologies used in onboard EV chargers are discussed in [10]. Most of these topologies involve a front-end rectifier and PFC, followed by a DC link and an isolated buck conversion stage. The most popular topology in this kind of system is the LLC resonant converter-based charger, as discussed in [11]. In [12], the high-frequency transformer analysis of another popular topology, the phase-shift full-bridge converter, is presented. Too many conversion stages and the DC bus in between reduce the overall system's efficiency [13]. Figure 1 shows a conventional DC link-based charging system with isolation. In this paper, a converter is used which converts AC to high-frequency AC for power factor correction, and it also provides a high-frequency output that can be isolated from the battery side using a high-frequency compact isolation transformer.In this PFC based isolated charger three back to back connected PI are used. Which together
1)
Convert the AC to DC require for battery $\text{CC}-\text{CV}$ charging 2)
Maintain the power factor and THD profile of the supply side. 3)
provide isolation between grid and the supply side. The most efficient and safe CC-CV method is used to charge the battery [14]. The PI controllers are designed using K factor method [15].
Currently in indian market the available four wheeler segment uses battery bank having around 320 V nominal battery voltage, so charger is designed four that segment. The specifications for this charger is given in Table 1. The paper is organized as follows: Section II presents the basic understanding of the topology used and the control strategies in detail. Section III provides the simulation results to verify the proposed technique. Finally, Section VI concludes the paper.

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Conventional DC link based Topology
sec.p:
Figure 2 shows the circuit of DC link based two stage EV charger. In this charger, there are two different control systems: one for power factor correction and another for CC-CV charging. Figure 4 shows the PFC control system for the circuit shown in Figure 2. In this, the PFC unit is responsible for maintaining the DC bus voltage and shaping the supply current (PFC). The DC bus voltage reference is compared with the actual DC bus voltage to generate an error, and a current reference is then generated. A PLL (phase-locked loop) is used to generate a sinusoidal reference for the inductor current. The unit vector from the PLL is multiplied by the output of the PI controller to generate the current reference. This reference current is then compared with the actual inductor current and fed to a PI controller, which produces a gate pulse for the switch (Sw). The other part of this conventional charger, shown in Figure 2, includes a high-frequency inverter, which converts DC to high-frequency AC. The high-frequency transformer then provides isolation between the grid side and the battery side, after which a rectifier converts it back into DC according to the battery charging requirements. This high-frequency inverter, isolation transformer, and rectifier, along with $L_{b}$ and $C_{o}$ form an isolated DC-DC converter that controls the voltage and current during CC-CV charging. Figure 5 shows the CC-CV charging control for the circuit shown in Figure 2. In Figure 5, two PI controllers are used. The $V_{\text {batref }}$ is the maximum allowable charging voltage for the battery. It is compared with the actual battery voltage, and if $V_{\text {bat }}$ is less than $V_{\text {batref }}$, the PI controller sets the current to its maximum constant current value. After the battery reaches its charging voltage and becomes equal to $V_{\text {batref }}$, it starts reducing the battery current.
Fig. 1.
Structure of ev charger
Fig. 2.
Conventional dc link based charger topology
Fig. 3.
Circuit of bidirectional charger
Fig. 4.
Control of boost pfc
Table I Specifications of bidirectional charger
Parameter Value
Ac Voltage(V) 230 V
Supply Frequency(Hz) 50
Grid Side Power Factor 0.99
Switching Frequency(KHz) 100
Power Rating 3 kW
Inductor $\mathrm{L}_{s}(m H)$ 1
Battery Nominal Voltage (V) 320
Capacitor $\mathrm{C}_{o}(\mu F)$ 5600
Ripple in Inductor Current (A) 20%
Ripple in Output Voltage (V) 5%
Fig. 5.
Cc-cv control of dc link based charger
Fig. 6.
Circuit of single stage ev charger
Fig. 7.
Switching pattern of single stage charger

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Single Stage Topology
sec.p:
The control used in the single-stage topology is derived by combining the two stages of the conventional DC link-based topology. Figure 6 shows the circuit diagram of the proposed topology, in which eight switches are connected in both directions to convert AC to high-frequency AC. In this circuit, only a single control system is used in place of two different control systems for the entire circuit control, and three PI controllers are used in total. Figure 7 shows the switching pattern of the single-stage charger during positive and negative 50 Hz AC supply. During the positive AC supply, only four switches - SF1, SF2, SF3, and SF4 are in active mode. The switching frequency of the PWM signal is double that of the high-frequency AC. As shown in Figure 7, during the first half-cycle of the high-frequency AC output (when Vac is positive), all four switches are turned on, and the inductor starts storing energy. Later, SF2 and SF3 are forced to turn off, and the voltage is connected to the transformer through SF1 and SF4 during this period. Similarly, for the negative high-frequency cycle, SF2 and SF3 remain on to supply power to the battery through the transformer while the inductor releases energy.Similarly, for the negative cycle of the supply voltage, the other four switches become active. Figure 8 shows the control diagram of the single-stage topology for CC-CV charging. In this single-stage charger, the boost mode of operation is used. Similar to a boost converter, during the on-time, the inductor stores energy, and during the offtime, it supplies energy to the battery. The inductor handles both energy storage and input current shaping, so it can be concluded that the inductor current is not a direct source of energy to the battery.In this control system, there are three back-to-back connected controllers. Thus, the supply current must be maintained to keep the battery voltage and current as per the requirements, while ensuring its waveform is sinusoidal. $V_{\text {batref }}$ is compared with $V_{\text {bat }}$, and $I_{\text {batref }}$ is generated. This process is similar to the CC-CV charging used for DC link-based chargers. $V_{b a t}$ denotes the maxi-mum allowable battery voltage, and $i_{\text {bat }}$ is saturated at its constant current (CC) value. When the battery is discharged, $V_{\text {bat }}$ is always less than $V_{\text {batref }}$, causing the controller to start charging in CC mode. When $V_{\text {bat }}$ reaches $V_{\text {batref }}$, the controller starts reducing the battery current. A PI controller is designed between $I_{\text {bat }}$ and $I_{L}$ to generate $I_{\text {Lref }}$. As shown in Figure 8, the output of this PI controller is multiplied by the output of the PLL to make its shape sinusoidal. This current reference is then compared with the actual current, and the last PI controller generates the PWM signals. Table 2 explain the details of all three control loos used in this system.
Fig. 8.
Control system of single stage ev charger
Table II Specifications of bidirectional charger
Loop Purpose Band Width inputs output
Outer Voltage Control Loop (CV Loop) Controls Output Voltage in CV mode 20 Hz Battery CV threshold Voltage $V_{\text {Batref }}$ and Battery Measured Voltage $V_{\text {Bat }}$ Battery Current Reference $I_{\text {Batref }}$
Middle Battery Current Control Loop (CC Loop) Controls Battery Charging current in CC mode 500 Hz Battery Current Reference $I_{\text {Batref }}$ and Measured Battery Current $I_{\text {Bat }}$ Inductor Current Reference $I_{\text {Lref }}$
Inner Inductor Current Control Loop (Inner PFC Loop) Regulates inductor current for PFC 5 KHz Inductor Current Reference $I_{\text {Lref }}$ and Measured Inductor Current $I_{L}$ PWM Signal
Fig. 9.
Control system of single stage ev charger
Fig. 10.
Control system of single stage ev charger
Fig. 11.
Control system of single stage ev charger

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Simulation Study
sec.p: The simulation of a single-stage isolated EV charger is done in MATLAB Simulink software. Figure 12 shows the AC supply current and voltage, which are in phase with each other. Figure 13 shows the THD analysis of the supply current. In Figure 14, the CC-CV charging of the battery is shown, in which the charging voltage is set to 338 volts and the constant current is set to 8.5 amps. To reduce the simulation time, a variable resistance is used in place of the battery, and its value is varied continuously to observe the transition from CC to CV charging.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p:
In this article, the working and control of a single-stage onboard charger is discussed, along with PFC. The power factor is maintained close to unity, and the THD is approximately 2.1 %, which is within acceptable limits as per IEC standards. The $\text{CC}-\text{CV}$ operation of the charger is also verified.
Fig. 12.
Input side voltage and current of single stage ev charger
Fig. 13.
Thd analysis of input side current
Fig. 14.
Cc-cv charging of single stage ev charger

ack.title: Acknowledgment
ack.p: Authors would like to acknowledge Department of Science and Technology, Government of India for the financial support under EU-India RE-EMPOWERED Project (File No.: DST/TMD/INDIA/EU/ILES/2020/50).
